scoped_disciplines.label: constitutional history
scoped_disciplines.rationale: Directly traces the evolution of Roman Republican institutions, magistracies, and constitutional arrangements as expressions of political power.

scoped_disciplines.label: constitutional law
scoped_disciplines.rationale: Covers the formal structures of Republican governance — consular authority, senatorial supremacy, and the distribution of sovereign power.

scoped_disciplines.label: civil and political rights
scoped_disciplines.rationale: Citizenship as a political status (civitas) was the foundational category determining participation in Roman Republican governance.

scoped_disciplines.label: geopolitics
scoped_disciplines.rationale: The Republic's expansion, provincial governance, and interstate relations with Carthage, Greece, and the East are core political phenomena.

scoped_disciplines.label: economic history
scoped_disciplines.rationale: Included only insofar as fiscal policy, grain supply (annona), and land redistribution debates (Gracchan reforms) were instruments of political contestation.

scoped_disciplines.label: auxiliary science of history
scoped_disciplines.rationale: Provides methodological tools (epigraphy, numismatics as political propaganda) for reconstructing Republican political history.

scoped_disciplines.label: administrative law
scoped_disciplines.rationale: Provincial administration and magisterial imperium represent political control mechanisms, making administrative law politically relevant here.

scoped_lcc.label: Political theory — General and comparative
scoped_lcc.rationale: Covers theories of sovereignty, republicanism, and political authority directly applicable to the Roman Republic's governmental model.

scoped_lcc.label: Geopolitics
scoped_lcc.rationale: Encompasses Roman territorial expansion, hegemony over Italy, and imperial projection as political phenomena.

scoped_lcc.label: Civil and political rights
scoped_lcc.rationale: Political citizenship (civitas Romana) and its gradual extension were central political events of the Republican period.

scoped_lcc.label: Political institutions — Europe
scoped_lcc.rationale: Houses scholarship on Roman Republican constitutional arrangements and their European institutional legacy.

scoped_lcc.label: History of Italy — Rome (Ancient)
scoped_lcc.rationale: The primary LCC location for historical and political narrative of the Roman Republic, including senatorial politics and civil wars.

scoped_lcc.label: Law — General
scoped_lcc.rationale: Included selectively where law functions as a political weapon — e.g., quaestiones perpetuae and proscription legislation.

scoped_lcc.label: Constitutional history and law — Ancient
scoped_lcc.rationale: Directly relevant to the formal constitutional arrangements of the Roman Republic including the cursus honorum and tribunicia potestas.

scoped_lcsh.label: Rome — History — Republic, 510–30 B.C.
scoped_lcsh.rationale: The canonical LCSH heading precisely delimiting the political history of the Roman Republic as a discrete governmental era.

scoped_lcsh.label: Constitutional history
scoped_lcsh.rationale: Covers the historical development of Republican constitutional structures including consuls, Senate, tribunes, and popular assemblies.

scoped_lcsh.label: Constitutional law
scoped_lcsh.rationale: Formal legal-political framework of Republican magistracies, vetoes, and the lex as an instrument of political sovereignty.

scoped_lcsh.label: Civil and political rights
scoped_lcsh.rationale: Citizenship extension (Social War, Latin rights) was a defining political conflict of the late Republic.

scoped_lcsh.label: Geopolitics
scoped_lcsh.rationale: Roman Republican foreign policy, provincial creation, and interstate dominance are geopolitical expressions of political power.

scoped_lcsh.label: Diplomatic history
scoped_lcsh.rationale: Treaties, alliances (foedera), and Roman diplomatic supremacy were political instruments of the Republic's power system.

scoped_lcsh.label: History, Military
scoped_lcsh.rationale: Included only where military command (imperium) and triumphs functioned as political power resources, not for operational military history per se.

scoped_lcsh.label: History
scoped_lcsh.rationale: General historical heading anchoring the Republican period's political narrative within the broader Roman historical record.

scoped_lcsh.label: Colonies — History
scoped_lcsh.rationale: Roman colonization was a deliberate political tool for controlling territory and extending citizen communities.

scoped_lcsh.label: Historical geography
scoped_lcsh.rationale: The political map of Republican Rome — provincial boundaries, allied territories, and voting districts (tribus) — is essential political geography.

scoped_entities.label: Roman Republic
scoped_entities.rationale: The primary subject entity — an aristocratic republic representing a distinct form of government and historical political period.

scoped_entities.label: Roman Senate
scoped_entities.rationale: The dominant political institution of the Republic, controlling foreign policy, finance, and provincial governance.

scoped_entities.label: Citizens' assemblies of the Roman Republic
scoped_entities.rationale: The comitia and concilium plebis were the sovereign legislative and electoral bodies expressing popular political will.

scoped_entities.label: Roman emperor
scoped_entities.rationale: Relevant as the institutional endpoint whose emergence marks the political collapse of the Republican system.

scoped_entities.label: Ancient Rome
scoped_entities.rationale: The encompassing political civilisation within which the Republican form of government operated and evolved.

scoped_entities.label: Rome
scoped_entities.rationale: The seat of Republican political institutions — Senate, Forum Romanum, and all central magistracies were located here.

scoped_entities.label: Caesar's Civil War
scoped_entities.rationale: The defining political crisis that terminated the Republican constitution and demonstrated the failure of its power-sharing mechanisms.

scoped_entities.label: Ancient Carthage
scoped_entities.rationale: Rome's primary political and military rival, whose defeat structured Roman Republican imperialism and provincial expansion.

scoped_entities.label: Deposition of Romulus Augustulus
scoped_entities.rationale: Marks the formal end of the Western Roman political tradition descended from the Republic, relevant as a terminal political boundary.

scoped_entities.label: Carthage
scoped_entities.rationale: The city whose political destruction (146 BCE) marked Rome's unchallenged political hegemony in the Mediterranean.

scoped_entities.label: Athens
scoped_entities.rationale: Politically significant as a model for Republican constitutional thought and as a subject of Roman political subordination after 146 BCE.

scoped_entities.label: Byzantium
scoped_entities.rationale: Relevant as a Greek polis under Republican political control and later as the site of imperial political continuity.

output.sfa_rationale: The POLITICAL facet of the Roman Republic centres on its distinctive form of government — an aristocratic republic governed through the Senate, magistracies, and popular assemblies — and the political dynamics of its rise, internal contestation, and fall. The scope prioritises governance structures (constitutional history, senatorial authority, citizen assemblies), political rights (civitas, the extension of the franchise), and geopolitical power projection (provincialisation, foreign policy, civil wars) as instruments and expressions of political power. Items primarily belonging to legal doctrine, social stratification, artistic production, or military operations are excluded unless they served as direct mechanisms of political authority — such as quaestiones as political battlegrounds or military imperium as a political resource.
